perturbation.original_text: Sponsor shall during the term of this Agreement -------------------- receive the following sponsorship benefits:
perturbation.changed_text: Client shall during the duration of this arrangement -------------------- receive the following sponsorship benefits:
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Sponsor' is changed to 'Client', creating uncertainty whether the defined entity 'Sponsor' is the same as 'Client'. This is especially impactful where the contract refers specifically to actions and responsibilities of the 'Sponsor' in later clauses, creating ambiguity. The in-text contradiction happens because the document started by defining the Sponsor as Mercata INC, but the term client is undefined and there is no clue whether the entity is still Mercata INC or a different entity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of the sponsorship rights and other services ------------- and products granted and provided to Sponsor by FNW hereunder, Sponsor hereby agrees to pay FNW a sponsorship fee in the amount of Forty thousand Dollars ($40,000 net) (the "Sponsorship Fee").
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Sponsor' to 'Client' creates a misaligned terminology contradiction. The contradicted text explicitly refers to 'Sponsor' agreeing to pay fees. If 'Sponsor' is no longer definitively defined (due to the introduction of the undefined 'Client'), it creates ambiguity about which entity is obligated to pay, directly conflicting with the clear obligation stated in the contradicted section. The definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction directly applies.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence on July 19, 1999 and shall ---- thereafter continue until all above described Sponsor benefits are completed, but in no event beyond the end of the 1999 season (the "Term").
perturbation.changed_text: The duration of this arrangement shall commence on July 19, 1999 and shall ---- thereafter continue until all above described Vendor benefits are completed, but in no event beyond the end of the 1999 season (the "Term").
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Sponsor benefits' is changed to 'Vendor benefits'. This introduces a contradiction because previously the agreement clearly stated the entity that gets the benefits is the 'Sponsor', but no entity was defined to be the 'Vendor'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of the sponsorship rights and other services ------------- and products granted and provided to Sponsor by FNW hereunder, Sponsor hereby agrees to pay FNW a sponsorship fee in the amount of Forty thousand Dollars ($40,000 net) (the "Sponsorship Fee").
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "Sponsor benefits" with "Vendor benefits" while the contradicted text refers to "Sponsor". The explanation states that the agreement defines 'Sponsor' but not 'Vendor'. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text explicitly states that the Sponsor receives rights and services, and in consideration, pays a sponsorship fee. By changing "Sponsor benefits" to "Vendor benefits", the agreement now implies the Vendor receives the benefits originally intended for the Sponsor. This contradicts the section defining the Sponsorship Fee as payment for benefits given to the Sponsor.

perturbation.original_text: Sponsor may use Seahawks trademarks for promotional purposes as necessary to convey the Sponsor benefits described herein. However, all such rights shall expire at the end of the 1999 regular season.
perturbation.changed_text: Advertiser may use Seahawks trademarks for promotional purposes as necessary to convey the Advertiser benefits described herein. However, all such rights shall expire at the end of the 1999 regular season.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Sponsor' is replaced with 'Advertiser'. This causes confusion about which entity is allowed to use the Seahawks trademarks, given that 'Sponsor' is explicitly defined as 'MERCATA, Inc.,' and 'Advertiser' is not defined at all. There is no clue on whether the advertiser is the same Sponsor entity defined in the begining of the text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: FNW agrees to defend, indemnify and hold Sponsor harmless --------------- from and against all claims, suits, liabilities, costs and expenses, including reasonable attorney costs and fees, for injury to, including death of, persons (whether they be third persons or employees of either of the parties hereto) or any loss of or damage to property in any manner arising from or relating to the rights 3. conveyed herein, with the understanding that this obligation shall not apply to, and Sponsor agrees to defend, indemnify and hold FNW and its officers, directors, employees and agents harmless from and against, all losses, claims, suits, demands, actions, liabilities, costs and expenses, including reasonable attorney costs and fees, for injury to, including death of, persons (whether they be third persons or employees of either of the parties hereto) or any loss of or damage to property in any manner arising from the content of any advertising copy supplied by Sponsor or the negligence or intentional misconduct of Sponsor or its officers, employees or agents.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly refers to 'Sponsor' which is defined elsewhere in the contract as 'MERCATA, Inc.'. The changed text replaces 'Sponsor' with 'Advertiser', an undefined term. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text indemnifies 'Sponsor'. Since 'Advertiser' is not defined, it's unclear if the indemnification clause still applies to the entity now permitted to use the trademarks, leading to ambiguity and a potential conflict. The 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition is met.
